title: Apply: Slavery: An Overview
description: Apply your understanding of the history of the institution of slavery in the United States with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Article I, Section 9 of the U.S. Constitution.**

The Migration or Importation of such Persons as any of the States now existing shall think proper to admit, shall not be prohibited by the Congress prior to the Year one thousand eight hundred and eight, but a Tax or duty may be imposed on such Importation, not exceeding ten dollars for each Person.

**In this section of the Constitution, “Persons” refers to which group?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enslaved people
Immigrants
Indentured servants
Native Americans

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from James Madison’s July 9th notes on the debates in the Federal Convention of 1787.**

*Mr. Patterson* considered the proposed estimate for the future according to the Combined rule of numbers and wealth, as too vague. For this reason New Jersey was against it. He could regard negro slaves in no light but as property. They are no free agents, have no personal liberty, no [[☃ definition 1]] of acquiring property, but on the contrary are themselves property, and like other property entirely at the will of the Master. Has a man in Virginia a number of votes in proportion to the number of his slaves? And if Negroes are not represented in the States to which they belong, why should they be represented in the General Government? What is the true principle of Representation?

**Which constitutional compromise was Mr. Patterson opposed to, based on his comments at the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: James Madison, “Monday, July 9 | Debates in the Federal Convention of 1787,” *Teaching American History,* [Link to source](https://teachingamericanhistory.org/resource/the-constitutional-convention/debates/0709-2/)

choices.content: The Fugitive Slave Clause
The Great Compromise
The Three-Fifths Compromise
The Electoral College

**Based on the chart, how did the percentage of enslaved people affect the number of representatives in the House of Representatives?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: University of Maryland, Baltimore County, “Slavery, Free Black, and White Population, 1780-1830,” [Link to source](https://userpages.umbc.edu/~bouton/History407/SlaveStats.htm)

choices.content: Southern states would have fewer representatives than northern states if enslaved people were not included in the population count.
Southern states did not have a significant enslaved population, meaning that including them would not affect representation in the U.S. House.
Northern states had similar numbers of enslaved people included in their population counts.
Northern states had larger populations than  southern states, even after including all enslaved people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from The Republican Federalist V by James Warren, as published in *Massachusetts Centinel* in 1788.**

“Representatives” then are to be “apportioned among the several States, according to their respective numbers,” and *five* slaves, in composing those numbers, are to be classed with *three* freemen—By which rule, fifty thousand slaves, having neither *liberty or property,* will have a representative in that branch of the legislature—to which more especially will be committed, the *protection of the liberties,* and *disposal of all the property* of the freemen of the Union—for thus stands the new Constitution. 

Should it be said, that not *the slaves* but their *masters* are to send a representative, the answer is plain—If the *slaves* have a *right* to be represented, they are *on a footing* with freemen, *three* of *whom* can then have no more than an equal right of representation with *three slaves,* and these when qualified by property, may elect or be elected representatives, which is not the case: But if they have not a right to be represented, their masters can have no right derived from their *slaves,* for *these* cannot transfer to others what they have not themselves.

**Which statement best describes Warren’s argument in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It is unfair to the northern states not to tax the slave trade, as all property should be taxed.
The products created by enslaved labor are taxed, making it unnecessary to tax the slave trade.
Enslaved people should not be counted for the purpose of representation if they do not have a right to representation.
Taxes are morally wrong and affect productive northern states more than southern states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from The Republican Federalist V by James Warren, as published in *Massachusetts Centinel* in 1788.**

“Representatives” then are to be “apportioned among the several States, according to their respective numbers,” and *five* slaves, in composing those numbers, are to be classed with *three* freemen—By which rule, fifty thousand slaves, having neither *liberty or property,* will have a representative in that branch of the legislature—to which more especially will be committed, the *protection of the liberties,* and *disposal of all the property* of the freemen of the Union—for thus stands the new Constitution. 

Should it be said, that not *the slaves* but their *masters* are to send a representative, the answer is plain—If the *slaves* have a *right* to be represented, they are *on a footing* with freemen, *three* of *whom* can then have no more than an equal right of representation with *three slaves,* and these when qualified by property, may elect or be elected representatives, which is not the case: But if they have not a right to be represented, their masters can have no right derived from their *slaves,* for *these* cannot transfer to others what they have not themselves.

**Which section of the Constitution is Warren objecting to in his letter?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Slave Trade Clause
The Three-Fifths Clause
The Fugitive Slave Clause
The Elections Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from James Madison’s notes on the debates in the Federal Convention of 1787.**

But he [[☃ definition 1]] that the States were divided into different interests not by their difference of size, but by other circumstances; the most [[☃ definition 2]] of which resulted partly from climate, but principally from the effects of their having or not having slaves. These two causes [[☃ definition 3]] in forming the great division of interests in the U. States. It did not lie between the large & small States: It lay between the Northern & Southern, and if any defensive power were necessary, it ought to be mutually given to these two interests. 

He was so strongly impressed with this important truth that he had been casting about in his mind for some [[☃ definition 4]] that would answer the purpose. The one which had occurred was that instead of proportioning the votes of the States in both branches, to their respective numbers of inhabitants computing the slaves in the ratio of 5 to 3, they should be represented in one branch according to the number of free inhabitants only; and in the other according to the whole [number,] counting the slaves as if free. By this arrangement the Southern Scale would have the advantage in one House, and the Northern in the other.

**Which statement best summarizes Madison’s notes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: Size differences between the states caused significant conflict, and the boundaries of the states needed to be redrawn.
Slavery was a major source of conflict during the Constitutional Convention, and the country needed to decide whether to continue or end the practice of slavery.
Slavery was one of the most divisive issues between the states, and there needed to be compromises to form a Union that included both slaveholding and free states.
State size was determined by the primary industry of each state, and it was not up to the federal government to interfere on the issue.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from The Northwest Ordinance of 1787.**

There shall be neither slavery nor involuntary servitude in the said territory, otherwise than in the punishment of crimes whereof the party shall have been duly convicted: Provided, always, That any person escaping into the same, from whom labor or service is lawfully claimed in any one of the original States, such fugitive may be lawfully reclaimed and [[☃ definition 1]] to the person claiming his or her labor or service as aforesaid.

**The language in this section of the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 inspired which part of the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Article I, Section 2 (The Three-Fifths Clause)
Article I, Section 9 (The Slave Trade Clause)
Article IV, Section 1 (The Full Faith and Credit Clause)
Article IV, Section 2 (The Fugitive Slave Clause)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Benjamin Franklin’s 1790  letter to Vice President John Adams.**

Your Memorialists, particularly engaged in attending to the Distresses arising from Slavery, believe it their [[☃ definition 1]] Duty to present this Subject to your notice—They have observed with great Satisfaction that many important and [[☃ definition 2]] Powers are [[☃ definition 3]] you for “promoting the Welfare and securing the blessing of liberty to the People of the United States.” And as they [[☃ definition 4]]; that these blessing ought rightfully to be administered, without distinction of Colour, to all descriptions of People, so they indulge themselves in the pleasing expectation, that nothing which can be done for the relief of the unhappy objects of their care, will be either omitted or delayed.

From a persuasion that equal liberty was originally the Portion, and is still the Birthright of all Men, and influenced by the strong ties of Humanity and the Principles of their Institution, your Memorialists conceive themselves bound to use all justifiable [[☃ definition 5]] to loosen the bands of Slavery and promote a general Enjoyment of the blessings of Freedom.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The abolition of slavery is inevitable, so the federal government should end it now.
The issue of slavery will continue to cause conflict until either some states secede or the federal government bans it.
The Constitution does not guarantee liberty for all people, so the institution of slavery does not need to end.
To meet the promises stated in the Constitution, the institution of slavery must end.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Benjamin Franklin’s 1790  letter to Vice President John Adams.**

Your Memorialists, particularly engaged in attending to the Distresses arising from Slavery, believe it their [[☃ definition 1]] Duty to present this Subject to your notice—They have observed with great Satisfaction that many important and [[☃ definition 2]] Powers are [[☃ definition 3]] you for “promoting the Welfare and securing the blessing of liberty to the People of the United States.” And as they [[☃ definition 4]]; that these blessing ought rightfully to be administered, without distinction of Colour, to all descriptions of People, so they indulge themselves in the pleasing expectation, that nothing which can be done for the relief of the unhappy objects of their care, will be either omitted or delayed.

From a persuasion that equal liberty was originally the Portion, and is still the Birthright of all Men, and influenced by the strong ties of Humanity and the Principles of their Institution, your Memorialists conceive themselves bound to use all justifiable [[☃ definition 5]] to loosen the bands of Slavery and promote a general Enjoyment of the blessings of Freedom.

**Which founding document does Benjamin Franklin cite in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Declaration of Independence
The Constitution
The Articles of Confederation
The Northwest Ordinance

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a petition that four Black men submitted to the House of Representatives in 1797. They were challenging a North Carolina law that allowed for the capture and sale of any person who had been freed from slavery without court approval.**

If, notwithstanding all that has been publicly [[☃ definition 1]] as essential principles respecting the extent of human right to freedom; notwithstanding we have had that right restored to us, so far as was in the power of those by whom we were held as slaves, we cannot claim the privilege of representation in your councils, yet we trust we may address you as fellow-men, who, under God, the sovereign Ruler of the Universe, are entrusted with the distribution of justice. . . .  

We are far from considering all those who retain slaves as willful oppressors, being well assured that [many people] in the State from whence we are exiles, hold their slaves in bondage, not of choice, but possessing them by inheritance, feel their minds burdened under the [[☃ definition 2]] restraint of legal [[☃ definition 6]] to doing justice which they are convinced is due to [[☃ definition 3]]. May we not be allowed to consider this stretch of power, morally and politically, a Governmental defect, if not a direct violation of the declared fundamental principles of the Constitution; and finally, is not some remedy for an evil of such magnitude highly worthy of the deep inquiry and [[☃ definition 4]] [[☃ definition 5]] of the supreme Legislative body of a free and enlightened people?

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the petition?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: other people who are equally capable of reasoning

choices.content: The North Carolina law violates fundamental rights and must be addressed by the legislature, even though the petitioners have been denied representation.
Because the Constitution does not legalize slavery at the federal level, the House cannot declare the law unconstitutional.
The North Carolina law seeks to make the condition of slavery inheritable, passing from mother to child, which violates the natural rights of Black people.
Because of the defects in the structure of the federal government, the House may be unable to address the North Carolina law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In 1797, the House of Representatives received a petition from four free Black men challenging a North Carolina law. The allowed for the capture and sale of any person who had been freed from slavery without court approval. This is an excerpt from the House’s debate about whether they would hear the petition.**

Mr. George THATCHER (Massachusetts Representative) thought the petition ought to be referred to the Committee on the Fugitive Law. He [[☃ definition 1]] the gentleman much mistaken in asserting these petitioners to be absolute slaves. They state that they were slaves, but that their masters [[☃ definition 2]] them, and that their manumissions were [[☃ definition 3]] by a law of that State, but that a subsequent law of the same State, subjected them to slavery; and if even there was a law that allowed them to be taken and sold into slavery again, he could not see any [[☃ definition 4]] in refusing their petition in that House ⎯ THEY CERTAINLY (said Mr. T.) ARE FREE PEOPLE. . . . He could not see how there would be a propriety in rejecting their petition; they had an undoubted right to petition the House, and to be heard. . . . 

Mr. William SMITH (South Carolina Representative) [said] these men are slaves, and, he thought, not entitled to attention from that [[☃ definition 5]]; to encourage slaves to petition the House would have a tendency to invite continual applications. Indeed it would tend to spread an alarm throughout the Southern States; it would act as an “entering wedge,” whose consequences could not be foreseen. . . 

**The excerpt best illustrates which aspect of early America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the state of being appropriate or suitable

choices.content: The fact that the Constitution outlawed slavery
The different regional attitudes about the institution of slavery
The ways in which the framers compromised over the issue of slavery
The effect of the gag rule on slavery in the chambers of Congress

assessment_items.question_content:
**In 1797, the House of Representatives received a petition from four free Black men challenging a North Carolina law. The allowed for the capture and sale of any person who had been freed from slavery without court approval. This is an excerpt from the House’s debate about whether they would hear the petition.**

Mr. George THATCHER (Massachusetts Representative) thought the petition ought to be referred to the Committee on the Fugitive Law. He [[☃ definition 1]] the gentleman much mistaken in asserting these petitioners to be absolute slaves. They state that they were slaves, but that their masters [[☃ definition 2]] them, and that their manumissions were [[☃ definition 3]] by a law of that State, but that a subsequent law of the same State, subjected them to slavery; and if even there was a law that allowed them to be taken and sold into slavery again, he could not see any [[☃ definition 4]] in refusing their petition in that House ⎯ THEY CERTAINLY (said Mr. T.) ARE FREE PEOPLE. . . . He could not see how there would be a propriety in rejecting their petition; they had an undoubted right to petition the House, and to be heard. . . . 

Mr. William SMITH (South Carolina Representative) [said] these men are slaves, and, he thought, not entitled to attention from that [[☃ definition 5]]; to encourage slaves to petition the House would have a tendency to invite continual applications. Indeed it would tend to spread an alarm throughout the Southern States; it would act as an “entering wedge,” whose consequences could not be foreseen. . . 

**The debate in the excerpt most directly reflects the constitutional question in which Supreme Court case?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the state of being appropriate or suitable

choices.content: *Marbury v. Madison* (1803)
*Dred Scott v. Sandford* (1857)
The *Slaughterhouse Cases* (1873)
*Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896)
